sourceData.description: The first AI agent harness native to the browser. A Chrome/Firefox extension that runs the agent loop in your browser — drives your tabs, spins up sandboxed compute (JS notebooks, WASM Linux VMs, client-side apps), and shares what it builds peer-to-peer. bodyHtml:
peerd is making waves in the Frontend ecosystem this week, gaining 0 new stars. With a total of 197 stars on GitHub and 17 forks, this open-source project is rapidly becoming a go-to tool for developers worldwide.

⭐ 197Stars
📈 +0/wkGrowth
🔀 17Forks
⚠️ 9Open Issues

Key Features

  • Written in JavaScript for maximum performance
  • Licensed under Apache-2.0 — free to use and modify
  • Official website: https://peerd.ai
  • 🏷️ agentic
  • 🏷️ ai-agent
  • 🏷️ browser-extension
  • 🏷️ chrome-extension
  • 🏷️ firefox-extension

Why peerd Is Trending

The Frontend landscape is constantly evolving, and peerd has emerged as one of the fastest-growing projects this week. Several factors contribute to its momentum:

  1. Growing community: With 0 new stars this week alone, developer adoption is accelerating rapidly.
  2. Active maintenance: The project sees regular updates, ensuring compatibility with the latest ecosystem changes.
  3. Practical utility: The first AI agent harness native to the browser. A Chrome/Firefox extension that runs the agent loop in your browser — drives your tabs, spins up sandboxed compute (JS notebooks, WASM Linux VMs, client-side apps), and shares what it builds peer-to-peer. BYOK · no backend · no telemetry.

If you're working in Frontend, this is definitely a project worth watching — and adopting.

Quick Start

Get started with peerd in minutes:

# Clone the repository git clone https://github.com/NotASithLord/peerd # Navigate to project cd peerd # Check the README for detailed setup instructions

For full documentation and advanced usage, visit the official GitHub repository.

Final Thoughts

peerd is a solid addition to any developer's toolkit. With its growing community, active development, and practical design, it's well worth your time to evaluate. Star it on GitHub, try it in a side project, and see if it fits your workflow.
